ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry specific to Myanmar rice noodle soup (htannyat dain gyat). FOODON_03315595 (noodle soup food product) used as closest parent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP (Noodle soups) used as closest parent; no Myanmar-specific rice noodle soup entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Myanmar rice noodle soup or htannyat dain gyat.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, representing a typical bowl of Myanmar rice noodle soup as served in a restaurant setting.

nutrient_source.title: Mohinga (Myanmar rice noodle fish soup) — nutrient composition estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table and USDA FoodData Central component data for rice noodles, fish broth, and condiments
nutrient_source.organization: FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asia Food Composition Table; USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for htannyat Myanmar dain gyat (Myanmar rice noodle soup) as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA component data for rice noodles and fish-based broth, cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy data for individual ingredients (rice vermicelli, fish stock, lemongrass, banana stem, chickpea flour, fish sauce, egg). Values represent a plausible per-100g composite for a typical bowl. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Rice noodles GI — analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al., 2008, Diabetes Care)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Myanmar htannyat dain gyat. GI estimated at 53 based on rice vermicelli (GI ~53, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database. The dish is predominantly rice noodle-based with a fish broth; protein and fat content from fish broth and egg moderately attenuate glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (53 × 10.5 × 4.0) / 100 = 22.3 g, using 400 g serving and 10.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and broth
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request reduced fish sauce, as a 400 g serving provides approximately 1520 mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that a standard 400 g bowl delivers approximately 22 g glycemic load from rice noodles; pairing with additional protein or reducing noodle portion can help moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from fish-based broth
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate consumption due to high sodium content (~1520 mg per serving) and moderate saturated fat; the fish protein component provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids that partially offset cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Myanmar rice noodle soup (ထန်းညက် မြန်မာဒိန်ချည်, romanized: htannyat Myanmar dain gyat) is a traditional Burmese noodle dish built on a foundation of thin rice vermicelli noodles (htannyat) served in a richly flavoured fish-based broth. The broth is typically prepared by simmering catfish or other freshwater fish with lemongrass, banana stem, onion, garlic, ginger, and chickpea flour, which gives it a characteristic slightly thick, savoury, and mildly earthy flavour. The dish is closely related to mohinga, Myanmar's national breakfast dish, and shares its core broth profile. Toppings commonly include a halved boiled egg, crispy split-pea fritters, fried garlic, coriander, and a squeeze of lime. The texture contrasts soft, slippery rice noodles with the crunchy fritters and the silky broth. Nutritionally, the dish provides moderate energy (~72 kcal per 100 g), meaningful protein from fish and egg (~4.2 g per 100 g), and low fat. The primary clinical concern is sodium content, which is elevated due to fish sauce and broth concentration (~380 mg per 100 g, approximately 1520 mg per standard 400 g bowl). The glycemic index is estimated at 53 (low category) based on rice vermicelli data, making it a moderate glycemic-load meal. It is dairy-free and generally halal. In Singapore, this dish is served at Myanmar restaurants such as Golden Cart Myanmar Restaurant and represents an authentic example of Burmese culinary heritage available in the diaspora food scene.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Myanmar rice noodle soup?
Is Myanmar noodle soup high in sodium?
What is the GI of rice noodle soup?
What are the ingredients in Burmese noodle soup?
Can diabetics eat Myanmar noodle soup?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific Myanmar rice noodle soup dish. Nutrient values are estimated from FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy component data for rice noodles, fish broth, and condiments. GI is estimated from rice vermicelli data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this estimation. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 4. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed zero as this is a fish-broth and rice noodle dish with no hydrogenated fats. 5. GI estimated at 53 (low) based on rice vermicelli analogue from University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this dish exists. 6. The food name in the input record (ထန်းညက် မြန်မာဒိန်ချည်) is the Burmese-script name for Myanmar rice noodle soup, romanized as htannyat Myanmar dain gyat. The canonical English name assigned is Myanmar Mohinga Noodle Soup. 7. Cuisine is Burmese; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. Burmese-script and romanized forms are provided. 8. Sodium content is high (~380 mg/100g); clinical guidance for hypertension and cardiovascular disease reflects this. 9. The cuisine_tag Lor Mee in the input is a loose descriptor used by the field survey platform; this dish is not Lor Mee but a Myanmar rice noodle soup. The tag is preserved in aliases for retrieval purposes only.
